data.title: Corn futures rise as USDA resumes release of export sales data
data.description:
U.S. corn futures rose Thursday after the U.S. Department of Agriculture released export sales data for the last two weeks of September, the first new export sales reports since the end of the government shutdown.

For the week ended September 18, the USDA reported corn sales totaled 1.92 million metric tons, soybean sales were 724,500 tons, and wheat sales came in at 539,900 tons.

For the week ended September 25, corn sales were 1.42 million tons, soybean sales were 870,500 tons, and wheat sales were 315,900 tons.

The USDA also said it will be publishing its daily sales of grains to private exporters that was not reported during the government shutdown, and traders hope the reports will say China was a customer for soybeans at any time from October to early November; analysts say no mention of China likely would bring selling pressure to CBOT grains, particularly soybeans.

The USDA said it will publish the sales made to private exporters on Friday, at the same time as the monthly WASDE report, which is expected to make cuts to previously forecast corn and soybean yields.

Market participants also are worried that China may have purchased so many soybeans from South America that an oversupply has been created, with no indications surfacing that China has followed through on promises to buy more U.S. soybeans.

The Trump administration said China had pledged to buy 12 million tons of soybeans by the end of this year, followed by 25 million tons annually over the next three years, but China has not confirmed any specific purchase commitments, although the government has reduced tariffs on U.S. soybeans and lifted import bans on three American exporters.

"Within the industry, many view the reported commitment by China to purchase 12 million tons of U.S. soybeans to be more of a diplomatic gesture than a firm trade deal," StoneX agricultural broker Kang Wei Cheang told Bloomberg this week.

CBOT corn (C_1:COM) for December delivery closed +1.6% to $4.42 1/4 per bushel, soybeans (S_1:COM) for January delivery ended +1.1% to $11.46 per bushel, and wheat (W_1:COM) for December delivery settled -0.1% to $5.35 1/4 a bushel.

ETFs: (CORN), (SOYB), (WEAT), (DBA), (MOO)

data.title: BioAtla outlines $800M peak sales target for Oz-V as FDA alignment paves way for phase III launch
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BioAtla, Inc. (BCAB) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Jay Short, Co-Founder, CEO & Chairman, emphasized that BioAtla is "currently in advanced stages to finalize a strategic transaction with a potential partner by year-end." He announced FDA alignment on the Phase III Oz-V registrational trial design, which includes dosing regimen, comparator arm, and approval endpoints for second-line plus oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (OPSCC). He highlighted that "this dual endpoint design provides the opportunity for achieving accelerated approval followed by full approval."
  • Short cited the presentation of "compelling data with multiple programs, including our Dual-CAB EpCAM TCE or BA3182 and Mec-V," and noted a development milestone achievement with Context Therapeutics for the dual CAB Nectin-4 TCE program, which brings non-dilutive capital and validates BioAtla's technology.
  • Sheri Lydick, Chief Commercial Officer, stated "we estimate worldwide peak sales of Oz-V to be approximately $800 million in second line and later OPSCC alone" and projects the total worldwide OPSCC market to reach $3 billion by 2032, with broader HPV-positive solid tumor market value exceeding $7 billion globally.
  • Eric Sievers, Chief Medical Officer, noted that "Oz-V's potential with our strong Phase II data in late-line patients, demonstrating an overall response rate of 45% and a median overall survival of 11.6 months compared to the historical response rates of only 0% to 3.4% and median overall survival of only 4.4 months with standard therapies."
  • Richard Waldron, Senior VP & CFO, reported "as of September 30, 2025, we had $8.3 million in cash and cash equivalents." He added that BioAtla received a $2 million milestone payment in October 2025 from Context Therapeutics and continued, "for the third quarter ended September 30, 2025, we reported a net loss of $15.8 million compared to a net loss of $10.6 million in the same quarter of 2024."

Outlook

  • Management stated preparations for the Oz-V Phase III study are ongoing and the company "remain[s] on track to advance this program" with the goal of beginning enrollment early next year.
  • The company expects "a key clinical trial readout in the first half of next year with several important events related to this program throughout 2026."
  • BioAtla continues to expect R&D expenses to decline through the remainder of 2025 as resources are concentrated on prioritized programs.

Financial Results

  • Waldron specified that R&D expenses were $9.5 million for the quarter ended September 30, 2025, compared to $16.4 million for the same quarter in 2024, attributing the decrease to "reduced program development costs due to prioritization of clinical programs, lower headcount-related expenses following the workforce reduction announced in March 2025 and lower noncash stock-based compensation."
  • General and administrative expenses were $4.2 million for the quarter, down from $5.9 million in the same quarter of 2024, due to "reduced personnel costs related to the workforce reduction in March 2025 and lower stock-based compensation expense."
  • The company reported a net loss of $15.8 million, with the increase compared to the prior year driven by lower collaboration revenue and a $2.1 million noncash loss on warrant liability, partially offset by lower operating expenses.

Q&A

  • Yu He, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about patient numbers for accelerated approval in the Phase III ROR2 study and stratification in the control arm. Eric Sievers responded that "for full approval, we're looking at approximately 300 patients that are prospectively randomized and stratified. And for accelerated approval, it would be an interim analysis roughly about the time of the full enrollment of patients, but... that look would be much earlier." Regarding stratification, Sievers mentioned "p16 would be one of them, and then it would have to do with local regional disease, yes or no."
  • Yu He, H.C. Wainwright: Inquired about the BA3182 program and expected data readouts. Sievers indicated that "for the next data output, we would anticipate it would be in the first half of next year, and we would be reporting pretty comprehensively on the additional dose and schedule evaluations."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral tone, focusing on clinical trial design, patient stratification, and timelines for data readouts, without expressing overt skepticism or enthusiasm.
  • Management's tone remained confident and optimistic in both prepared remarks and responses, with Short describing it as "a very exciting time for the company" and Sievers providing detailed, transparent responses.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analyst sentiment remained steady, with management retaining a forward-looking, confident stance and analysts continuing to probe for program details and timelines.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced a major update with FDA alignment on the Phase III Oz-V trial, while last quarter focused on ongoing FDA discussions and preparation for the meeting.
  • A projected $800 million peak sales figure for Oz-V was disclosed this quarter, providing new commercial guidance not previously stated.
  • The prior quarter highlighted partnership diligence at the term sheet stage, while the current quarter indicated advanced stages to finalize a strategic transaction by year-end.
  • R&D and G&A expenses continued to decline, following the workforce reduction and program prioritization first disclosed in Q2.
  • The tone of both management and analysts remained consistent, with management highlighting significant milestones and optimism, and analysts maintaining a detailed, factual line of questioning.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted the company had $8.3 million in cash and cash equivalents as of September 30, 2025, with additional milestone funding received in October.
  • The net loss increased due to lower collaboration revenue and a noncash warrant liability loss.
  • Decreased R&D and G&A expenses were attributed to prioritization of clinical programs and workforce reduction.
  • No new safety signals were identified in clinical programs, and the safety profile for lead assets remains manageable.

Final Takeaway

BioAtla emphasized that, with FDA alignment on the Oz-V Phase III trial, the company is prepared to launch a registrational study in early 2026 targeting the underserved OPSCC patient population. The management highlighted significant commercial potential, with Oz-V alone projected to reach $800 million in peak sales for second-line and later OPSCC. Ongoing progress in pipeline programs, continued cost reductions, and the anticipation of a strategic transaction by year-end were also highlighted as key drivers for the upcoming quarters.

data.title: Luminar signals strategic review and pivots toward defense and commercial markets amid automotive headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Luminar Technologies (LAZR) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Paul Ricci described Q3 2025 as a pivotal and challenging quarter, highlighting "deliberate action to reposition the company" in response to recent developments. Ricci disclosed that Luminar has entered into forbearance agreements with the majority of secured noteholders through November 24 and is negotiating for further extensions to address capital structure and liquidity needs.
  • Ricci stated, "our 2025 financial guidance remains suspended. We've also paused usage of our equity finance, credit and preferred stock programs while we work toward a comprehensive solution." Multiple preliminary proposals and indications of interest to purchase the company, or certain assets, are being evaluated.
  • The company announced board changes, adding Patricia Ferrari and Elizabeth Abrams as independent directors, both with backgrounds in banking and restructuring advisory. CFO transition was also disclosed: "this will be Tom Fennimore's final quarter with Luminar as CFO... I also want to take a moment to welcome our new CFO, Tom Beaudoin."
  • On the automotive LiDAR business, Ricci noted, "the future course of our relationship with Volvo will depend on the outcome of ongoing processes. We've made a claim for damages and paused further production commitments of Iris units pending resolution." He emphasized a strategic shift: "We're pursuing nonautomotive markets more deliberately in elevating the role of our LSI Photonics business where we see continued progress."
  • Nonautomotive momentum includes progress in aerospace and defense, with Ricci stating, "Luminar now works with nearly all major developments in terrestrial off-road autonomy, including Caterpillar... expanding into defense and industrial use cases." LSI Photonics, now about one-third of Luminar's annual revenue, generated roughly $18 million year-to-date.
  • Cost alignment efforts are underway, with Ricci disclosing a "reduction of roughly 25% of our workforce by year-end" and a supply chain review to match lower volume expectations.
  • CFO Thomas J. Fennimore stated, "Revenue for the quarter came in at $18.7 million, up about 20% sequentially and 21% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • The company has suspended 2025 financial guidance and paused equity, credit, and preferred stock financing programs while evaluating strategic alternatives and negotiating with noteholders. Ricci explained, "we anticipate further extensions as we continue to negotiate with our secured noteholders towards a longer-term solution to our capital structure and liquidity needs."
  • Luminar is advancing a strategic shift toward nonautomotive sectors, particularly commercial, defense, and industrial markets, where it sees "growing high-margin opportunities."

Financial Results

  • Luminar reported $18.7 million in revenue for Q3 2025, with approximately 5,400 Iris sensors shipped, the majority to Volvo. Fennimore attributed revenue growth to higher NRE revenue and sequential LSI revenue increases.
  • Gross loss was negative $8.1 million (GAAP) and negative $7.3 million (non-GAAP), with sequential improvement due to higher NRE revenue, lower inventory purchases after the Volvo program pause, and reduced warranty expense.
  • Non-GAAP operating expenses were $43 million, a 9% sequential and 29% year-over-year decline, primarily from lower R&D spend and ongoing cost actions.
  • Cash and marketable securities at quarter-end totaled $74 million. Free cash flow was negative $48.5 million, improved from Q2 and significantly below the year-ago period.

Q&A

  • Yan Dong, Deutsche Bank: Asked about the direction of strategic actions and indications of interest for company sale or asset sales. Ricci responded, "we have had interest in assets, business lines and in fact, the entire company and we're in the process of evaluating those as well as other financing interest."
  • Dong inquired about next-gen product development. Ricci replied, "No, it has not been put on hold. We've maintain critical engineering and related resources necessary to pursue the Halo architecture... and that work continues unabated."
  • Jash Patwa, JPMorgan: Asked about LSI size and momentum. Ricci discussed LSI's "deep technologies in various areas of Photonics... and there has been, as I've mentioned, strategic interest in that business as well."
  • Patwa followed up on platform partner engagement (e.g., NVIDIA). Ricci stated, "The company does continue to work with platform players. I don't have any updates on partnerships in that area today now."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on strategic direction, potential sale, and product roadmap, displaying a neutral tone by seeking clarity on restructuring and future plans.
  • Management’s sentiment was disciplined and transparent, emphasizing challenges but also potential in new markets. Ricci noted, "While this is undoubtedly a challenging period, we're approaching it with disciplined transparency and focus."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management were more focused on existential and strategic issues, with management maintaining a composed, if cautious, tone amid heightened uncertainty.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance is now suspended compared to last quarter’s lowered outlook for 2025 revenue and sensor shipments.
  • Strategic focus has shifted more decisively toward nonautomotive sectors, with LSI Photonics’ role elevated and concrete examples of defense and commercial wins detailed this quarter.
  • Board and executive changes were announced this quarter, while last quarter focused more on operational milestones and cost reduction.
  • Both quarters featured analyst questions about market diversification and partnerships, but Q3 showed greater urgency regarding liquidity, restructuring, and the possibility of asset or company sale.
  • Management’s confidence in nonautomotive markets and LSI was more pronounced in Q3, while optimism about automotive revenue recovery diminished.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ricci outlined the risk that "the uncertain status of [the Volvo] relationship will reduce or perhaps eliminate the expected volume and revenues from the EX90 and ES90 programs."
  • Suspension of guidance and ongoing negotiations with noteholders underscore material capital structure and liquidity risks.
  • Management is mitigating risks by reducing workforce, aligning cost base, and pausing further production for key programs pending resolution of disputes.
  • Analysts inquired about strategic alternatives and the stability of product development initiatives amidst restructuring.

Final Takeaway

Management is steering Luminar through a period of significant transition, suspending financial guidance and pursuing a strategic pivot toward defense, commercial, and industrial markets led by the LSI Photonics business. Automotive headwinds, especially with Volvo and Mercedes, have triggered deeper cost cuts, leadership changes, and active evaluation of potential asset sales or a company sale. The company is prioritizing liquidity stabilization and operational realignment, while maintaining critical product development in next-generation technology and emphasizing momentum in nonautomotive sectors as a foundation for future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Marchex outlines 10% revenue growth and 10%+ EBITDA margin targets for 2026 as Archenia acquisition advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Marchex (MCHX) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Executive Chairman Russell Horowitz emphasized a pivotal moment for Marchex, highlighting "we feel the company is at a very positive inflection point, both strategically and operationally" and cited progress in expanding the customer footprint, evolving product capabilities, and building sales momentum. Horowitz detailed plans to broaden the company's offerings beyond analytics, aiming to "accelerate delivering more comprehensive solutions that address higher value impact needs across the entire customer acquisition and optimization journey."
  • President Troy Hartless reported, "The third quarter represented continued progress with launching new products and accelerating sales bookings to our highest levels this year." Hartless noted completion of the technology platform migration is nearing, and expressed confidence in gaining visibility on increased sustainable sales growth moving into 2026.
  • CFO Brian Nagle stated, "Revenue for the third quarter of 2025 was $11.5 million, which is down from $11.7 million for the second quarter of 2025." Nagle attributed revenue changes to new sales and upsells, offset by legacy platform migration. He added, "We anticipate that our gross profit margins can continue to improve over time as we are carrying an overall lower cost structure going forward, which could enable meaningful future operating and financial leverage."
  • COO Frank Feeney announced an agreement in principle to acquire Archenia, a performance-based customer qualification and acquisition company, involving a $10 million convertible note and an earn-out of up to 4 million shares. Feeney described Archenia as enabling "customers to pay for verified AI-validated outcomes such as appointments, sales and high-intent conversations," and said the combined entity could target $15 million quarterly revenue run rates and annualized 15% to 20% revenue growth in 2026.

Outlook

  • Nagle reiterated, "we currently anticipate that both revenue and adjusted EBITDA will be sequentially lower in the fourth quarter of 2025, as compared to the third quarter of 2025," citing seasonality and migration impacts.
  • Looking ahead, Nagle said, "we currently believe that in the course of 2026, we can see revenue growth on a run rate basis in the 10% range from year-end levels." He also projected, "the combination of increasing revenue growth, combined with lower overall operating expenses can lead to adjusted EBITDA margins of 10% or more."
  • Feeney added, "Marchex believes that revenue run rates for the potential combined company are approximately $15 million quarterly or approximately $60 million annualized, which could grow in the 15% to 20% range in the course of 2026."

Financial Results

  • Nagle reported $11.5 million in revenue for Q3 2025, noting the sequential decline from $11.7 million in Q2 2025 was due to ongoing migration activities. He stated cash on the balance sheet decreased to $10.3 million from $10.5 million at the end of Q2 2025, primarily due to timing of customer payments.
  • Operating expenditures saw efficiencies, attributed to organizational realignment following technology platform initiatives. Nagle highlighted that improved operating leverage was evidenced by a "meaningfully improved sequential adjusted EBITDA."

Q&A

  • Ross Koller, Koller Capital, asked about total addressable market after the Archenia acquisition. Horowitz responded, "the opportunity and addressable market, when you look at the combined company's ability to sell insights, actions and outcomes is multiples of our existing one."
  • Koller questioned the trade-off between growth and profitability. Horowitz answered, "as we look at the acquisition and our ability to accelerate growth potentially to the extent it gets consummated, we're just focused on maximizing the revenue growth and maintaining some baseline positive adjusted EBITDA margins in that 10% or more range."
  • Koller inquired about growth from new versus existing customers. Horowitz indicated the $100 million revenue opportunity is "way more tangible and way more achievable much sooner even just with the existing or installed base" if the deal is closed.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the tangible impact of the Archenia acquisition and pressed for clarity on addressable market, growth versus profitability, and installed base potential, reflecting a slightly positive but probing tone.
  • Management maintained a confident stance, frequently referencing inflection points and growth opportunities. Horowitz used phrases such as "inflection point," "significant opportunity," and "well positioned," reinforcing optimism. During Q&A, responses remained direct and confident.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone has become more assertive with forward-looking statements about market opportunity and combined company scale, while analysts shifted from pipeline and bookings questions to a stronger focus on acquisition impact and financial scalability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q3 2025 call introduced the proposed Archenia acquisition, a major new development absent in the previous quarter.
  • Q3 guidance continues to anticipate lower sequential revenue and adjusted EBITDA for Q4 2025, consistent with the prior quarter's outlook, but with added specificity about 2026 growth targets and margin improvement.
  • Management's strategic focus shifted from product launches and platform migration in Q2 2025 to scaling bundled solutions and integrating outcome-based offerings.
  • Key metrics changed from highlighting positive net income in Q2 to adjusted EBITDA leverage and anticipated margin expansion in Q3.
  • Analyst questions evolved from bookings and product pipeline to detailed discussion about the acquisition's effect on market size and growth composition.

Risks and Concerns

  • Nagle acknowledged continued revenue migration dilution and the impact of seasonality as headwinds for Q4 2025 performance.
  • The company is still completing the migration of more than 1,000 customers onto the new platform, which may temporarily depress revenue.
  • The Archenia acquisition is subject to several conditions: audited financials, fairness opinion, and approval by a majority of disinterested stockholders. The closing, if achieved, is expected in the first half of 2026.
  • Feeney noted that combining the businesses brings "greater customer value, stickiness and risk mitigation," suggesting the acquisition is intended to manage concentration and churn risks.

Final Takeaway

Marchex management underscored a strategic inflection point, driven by new product momentum, sales bookings growth, and the anticipated acquisition of Archenia. With the platform migration nearing completion, the company projects run rate revenue growth in the 10% range and adjusted EBITDA margins of 10% or higher in 2026, targeting even greater growth if the acquisition closes. The leadership team believes the combined entity could rapidly approach a $100 million revenue opportunity and achieve Rule of 30 to 40 metrics, supported by bundled AI-driven solutions and expanded market reach.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Clipper Realty outlines continued record residential rent growth and 60% lease-up of Prospect House while navigating office lease transitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Clipper Realty Inc. (CLPR) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Bistricer stated that "our residential properties continue to perform very well due to continued high residential rental demand, excellent cash flow, overall rents are generally at all-time highs and continue to increase, and we are nearly fully leased." He highlighted that in the third quarter, new leases exceeded prior rents by over 14%, which was the same as the previous quarter, and noted the initial lease-up of Prospect House at 953 Dean Street, which came online in August and is approximately 60% leased with free market rents in excess of $88 per foot. He also pointed out ongoing efforts to bring office properties at 250 and 141 Livingston Street back to a cash flow position.
  • COO Jacob Bistricer reported strong residential leasing, with stabilized properties at 99% leased overall and rents at record levels. He noted, "Overall, new rental rates at residential properties in the third quarter exceeded previous rent by over 14% and renewals by 5%." He added that Tribeca House had 99% occupancy with new rents at $105 per foot, Clover House had 100% occupancy with new leases at $95 per foot, and Pacific House was 97% leased with free market rents of $82 per foot on new leases.
  • CFO Lawrence Kreider stated, "For the third quarter, we achieved flat revenues of $37.7 million versus $37.6 million last year. NOI of $20.8 million this quarter versus $21.8 million last year, a decrease of $1 million and AFFO of $5.6 million this quarter versus $7.8 million last year, a decrease of $2.2 million." He cited three unusual items impacting results: termination of the New York City lease at 250 Livingston Street, initial lease-up expenses at Prospect House, and the absence of results from the sold 10 West 65th Street property. Kreider added, "We have $26.1 million unrestricted cash and $30.6 million restricted cash at the end of the quarter."

Outlook

  • Management expects demand for residential leasing products to remain strong as the overall rental housing supply in New York City stays constrained and new development is discouraged. Jacob Bistricer said, "We expect demand for our residential leasing products to remain strong in the foreseeable future."
  • The company is focused on the "full lease-up of Prospect House, finalization of the 141 and 250 Livingston negotiations and capitalizing on other possibilities that may present themselves," according to CEO David Bistricer.

Financial Results

  • The third quarter saw flat revenues of $37.7 million and NOI of $20.8 million. AFFO was $5.6 million, reflecting declines primarily due to the loss of office lease revenue and initial lease-up costs at Prospect House.
  • Ongoing residential properties contributed a $2.4 million increase in revenue and a $1.5 million increase in NOI, partially offset by higher collection and payroll expenses.
  • The company maintained a dividend of $0.095 per share for the quarter, with payment scheduled for December 4, 2025.
  • At quarter-end, operating debt was 88% fixed at an average rate of 3.87% and an average duration of 3.7 years.

Q&A

  • There were no analyst questions during the call. CEO David Bistricer concluded by thanking participants and stating, "We look forward to speaking with you again next quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • No analyst sentiment was recorded as there were no questions or exchanges.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing strong residential performance and cautious optimism about future leasing and office negotiations.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady, focusing on operational efficiency and lease-up progress, but with increased attention to the impact of office lease terminations and stabilization of new developments.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In the previous quarter, management reported record NOI and AFFO, while the current quarter reflected flat revenues and declines in NOI and AFFO due to office lease loss and initial lease-up costs at Prospect House.
  • Both quarters highlighted record residential rents and high occupancy, but this quarter emphasized the transition at office properties and the ramp-up of Prospect House leasing.
  • The previous quarter noted the sale of 10 West 65th Street and strong cash generation, while this quarter focused more on property-level performance and the impact of recent portfolio changes.
  • Management's confidence in residential performance remained high, though attention to office exposure and new project stabilization increased.
  • Analysts did not ask questions in either quarter, so there was no shift in external sentiment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified the termination of the New York City lease at 250 Livingston Street as a significant event impacting results.
  • The initial lease-up at Prospect House resulted in an excess of expenses over revenue for the period.
  • The absence of results from the sold 10 West 65th Street property contributed to the decline in AFFO and NOI.
  • Management is focused on finalizing negotiations for office properties and optimizing occupancy, pricing, and expenses to address these risks.

Final Takeaway

Clipper Realty emphasized that residential properties continued to deliver strong performance, with rents at record highs and nearly full occupancy across the portfolio. The company is advancing the lease-up of the newly completed Prospect House, now 60% leased, and remains committed to optimizing its asset base amid shifting office dynamics and the impact of recent property sales. Management maintains a focus on operational efficiency and anticipates robust demand for its residential offerings in the constrained New York City market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Plug Power suspends DoE-backed hydrogen project - report
data.description:
Plug Power (PLUG) confirmed it is suspending plans to build six facilities to produce and liquefy zero- or low-carbon hydrogen, putting at risk the $1.66 billion federal loan guarantee it obtained in January during the final days of the Biden administration, The Washington Examiner reported Thursday in its "Daily on Energy" newsletter.

The hydrogen fuel produced at the facilities was intended to be used for fuel cell electric material handling equipment as well as the transportation and industrial sectors.

In its 10-Q filing earlier this week, Plug (PLUG) disclosed it is temporarily suspending activities related to its Department of Energy loan while it evaluates reallocation of capital, and acknowledged the decision would place the $1.66 billion at risk as the DoE could modify or fully cancel the funding.

"Plug's trajectory remains driven by strong global demand for its hydrogen solutions, and the company's plans do not depend on government funding," the company told the Washington Examiner. "This evolution reinforces Plug's commitment to operational efficiency, stakeholder value, and sustainable growth."

data.title: CV Sciences outlines new non-cannabinoid product launches in 2026 while adapting to regulatory changes
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CV Sciences, Inc. (CVSI) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Joseph Dowling opened the call by stating, "We are pleased with our progress as we move closer to profitability and positive cash flow." He reported revenue of $3.3 million for the quarter, slightly down from the previous quarter. Dowling highlighted that the company maintained its "#1 position in the natural product retail channel and have also increased our market share in this critical sales channel as well." He emphasized ongoing product innovation efforts, with a focus on diversifying into non-cannabinoid products and leveraging the acquisition of Elevated Softgels to in-source manufacturing and control costs.
  • Dowling explained the company is actively pursuing M&A opportunities to "increase our scale with greater revenue, but also to increase our cost efficiency," specifically seeking assets that enhance in-house manufacturing and supply chain control.
  • He described the regulatory environment as challenging, referencing new federal language restricting hemp products but noted, "the restrictive language will not go into effect for a 1-year period from today." Dowling reiterated the company's intent to engage with Congress, advocacy groups, and industry participants to advocate for balanced regulation.
  • CFO Joerg Grasser stated, "During the third quarter of 2025, we continue to make strong progress on our cost-saving initiatives. We are approaching positive cash flow and delivered an adjusted EBITDA loss of just $118,000 with a healthy 48.5% gross margin." Grasser detailed that products launched since early 2023 accounted for 39% of total revenue in Q3 and highlighted growth in the direct-to-consumer channel.

Outlook

  • Dowling confirmed, "we are planning to launch several new products in early 2026," including both cannabinoid and non-cannabinoid offerings under the +PlusHLTH brand, targeting both human and pet markets. He stressed the company's adaptation to regulatory developments and its goal to achieve improved cost efficiency and gross margin through Elevated Softgels integration.
  • The company expects "improved cost efficiency and gross margin directly from the integration of Elevated Softgels" and is investing in equipment and people to improve productivity at the Elevated facility.
  • Grasser said, "We expect modest cash used in the near term as we integrate acquisitions and anticipate generating positive operating cash in 2026."

Financial Results

  • Revenue for the quarter was $3.3 million, down from $3.6 million in Q2 and representing a 16% year-over-year decline, driven by a 16% drop in unit sales due to temporary out-of-stock issues and regulatory restrictions in certain states.
  • Gross margin stood at 48.5%, up from 46% a year ago but lower than Q2’s 50.9%. Grasser attributed the year-over-year gain to lower freight, fewer inventory losses, and a favorable product and channel mix, partially offset by higher costs in new subsidiaries.
  • Direct-to-consumer revenue accounted for 45.2% of total revenue, up from 42.8% a year ago, though slightly down from Q2.
  • SG&A was $1.8 million, a sequential and year-over-year decrease, mainly due to lower legal and professional expenses.
  • The company reported an operating loss of $0.3 million, roughly flat year-over-year and slightly higher than last quarter. Adjusted EBITDA was a loss of $118,000, compared to a positive $59,000 in Q2.
  • Net loss for the quarter was $0.4 million, compared to $0.3 million in Q2. Cash at quarter-end was $0.4 million. Inventory stood at $4.1 million, down from $4.9 million at year-end 2024.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE + There were no analyst questions during this call, as explicitly stated in the transcript.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts did not pose questions, so no analyst sentiment could be gauged.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing adaptability, cost control, and advocacy efforts, as seen in statements like "We remain a compelling investment opportunity for several reasons" and "We are optimistic about the long-term opportunity for our company to remain and be a competitive force in the health and wellness industry."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained consistent, focusing on cost efficiency, regulatory adaptation, and optimism about long-term growth, despite increased emphasis on regulatory risks this quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue declined from $3.6 million in Q2 to $3.3 million in Q3, with gross margin dropping from 50.9% to 48.5% sequentially.
  • SG&A expense further decreased, continuing the trend of cost control. Adjusted EBITDA moved from a positive $59,000 in Q2 to a loss of $118,000 in Q3. Net loss increased from $0.3 million to $0.4 million quarter-over-quarter.
  • The strategic focus shifted more toward regulatory advocacy and the planned launch of new non-cannabinoid products in early 2026, whereas the previous quarter highlighted new product launches in late 2025 and efficiency gains from in-sourcing.
  • Management confidence and optimism persisted, although there was a heightened focus on navigating new federal legislative risks impacting hemp-derived products.
  • Analysts remained absent from the Q&A, as in the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Dowling identified a new federal regulatory risk: "The funding legislation passed in the U.S. Senate to reopen the federal government included language that disallows the sale of hemp products with greater than 0.4 milligrams of THC per container." He stressed that this language will not take effect for one year, providing time for industry advocacy.
  • The company is responding by lobbying for "a balanced and responsible regulatory framework that preserves access to hemp-derived health and wellness products."
  • Grasser cited temporary out-of-stock issues and restrictive state regulations as drivers of unit sales and revenue declines.
  • Management is mitigating risks by streamlining operations, investing in in-house manufacturing, and diversifying product offerings, including non-cannabinoid products, to offset regulatory headwinds.

Final Takeaway

CV Sciences management highlighted progress toward profitability through cost control, market share gains, and a strategic shift to non-cannabinoid product launches in early 2026. The company is proactively addressing new federal regulatory risks with advocacy efforts while integrating acquisitions to improve cost efficiency and gross margin. These combined efforts are positioning CV Sciences to maintain its competitive standing and drive long-term shareholder value despite near-term revenue pressures and legislative uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sally Beauty targets $120M in cumulative cost savings by 2026 while launching Sally Ignited store refresh
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sally Beauty Holdings, Inc. (SBH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Denise Paulonis, President, CEO & Director, announced, "Fiscal 2025 was a meaningful year for the company, highlighted by strong operating and financial performance in the context of a rapidly changing and uncertain macro environment." She emphasized comparable sales growth of 1.3%, a 100 basis point gross margin expansion to 52.2%, an adjusted operating margin of 9.4%, and a 10% increase in adjusted diluted earnings per share to $0.55 for Q4. Full year adjusted diluted EPS reached $1.90, up 12%.
  • Paulonis highlighted a $275 million operating cash flow, $119 million debt paydown, and over $50 million in share repurchases. She also noted the successful launch of Sally Ignited, a comprehensive brand refresh, and the expansion of the marketplace strategy, including a new partnership with Uber Eats.
  • Key product category growth was led by color, with 7% growth in Q4 and 4% for the full year, and digital sales momentum, with Sally U.S. and Canada's e-commerce sales up 34% year-over-year in Q4. BSG segment saw innovation drive about 30% of total hair care sales in 2025.
  • Strategic priorities for 2026 include customer activation, digital value, product innovation, and expanding new growth pathways. The CEO stated, "Our mission is to ensure that the Sally brand emotionally connects with our customers while creating a discovery-focused omnichannel specialty beauty experience."
  • Marlo Cormier, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "We concluded the year with strong business momentum, enabling us to deliver fourth quarter and full year results ahead of our expectations on the top and bottom line. Our performance reflects our disciplined execution and commitment to long-term value creation."

Outlook

  • For fiscal 2026, Sally Beauty expects consolidated net sales between $3.71 billion and $3.77 billion, comparable sales flat to up 1%, adjusted operating earnings of $328 million to $342 million, and adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $2 to $2.10 per share. The company plans capital expenditures of about $100 million and free cash flow of approximately $200 million.
  • Q1 2026 guidance includes consolidated net sales of $935 million to $945 million, comparable sales approximately flat, adjusted operating earnings of $75 million to $80 million, and adjusted diluted EPS between $0.43 and $0.47.
  • Paulonis shared a three-year target through 2028: net sales growth of 1% to 3% annually, adjusted operating earnings growth of 3% to 5%, adjusted diluted EPS growth of at least 10%, and $90 million to $120 million in annual capital expenditures.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net sales for Q4 increased 1.3% to $947 million. Comparable sales rose 1.3%. Gross margin expanded 100 basis points to 52.2%. Adjusted SG&A for Q4 was $405 million, up $14 million year-over-year.
  • Global e-commerce sales increased 15% to $105 million, representing 11% of total net sales. Sally Beauty segment net sales grew 1.4% to $542 million, with comparable sales up 1.2%. Sally e-commerce sales rose 23% to $47 million. BSG segment net sales increased 1.1% to $406 million, with comparable sales up 1.4%.
  • Inventory at year-end was $988 million, down 5% from last year. Cash and cash equivalents totaled $149 million, with no outstanding borrowings under the revolver. Free cash flow for Q4 was $78 million.
  • The Fuel for Growth program delivered $46 million in incremental pretax benefits in 2025, with a cumulative run rate of $74 million since inception and a target of $120 million by end of 2026.

Q&A

  • Oliver Chen, TD Cowen, Research Division: Asked about divisional performance drivers, comp ticket trends, and guidance regarding occupancy leverage. Paulonis responded that color strength in both divisions was notable, with Sally color up 8% and BSG up 5%, and discussed customer activation and ongoing positive momentum. Cormier addressed occupancy leverage, expecting similar leverage to last year.
  • Chen inquired about the consumer environment, possible comp upside, and long-term growth leverage points. Paulonis cited resilience in Sally customers and stability in stylists, noting "consumers remain choiceful" and monitoring lower-income segments affected by government shutdowns. Cormier stated Fuel for Growth will continue to provide leverage.
  • Susan Anderson, Canaccord Genuity Corp.: Asked for an update on the Sally store remodel program. Paulonis explained that 30 stores had been refreshed, with higher dwell time and cross-category shopping. Fifty more stores are planned for fiscal 2026.
  • Anderson followed up on growth in color at Sally and customer behavior. Paulonis highlighted growth from new, reactivated, and existing customers, with Licensed Colorist OnDemand driving confidence and store visits.
  • Lauren (for Simeon Gutman), Morgan Stanley: Asked about confidence in long-term EBIT growth as Fuel for Growth winds down. Cormier pointed to ongoing supply chain optimization and own brand penetration.
  • Lauren asked about Sally segment traffic and ticket expectations for 2026. Paulonis projected improvements driven by performance marketing, personalization, and cross-category shopping.
  • Sydney Wagner, Jefferies: Asked about category growth trends underpinning long-term sales targets and promotional activity. Paulonis expects color to remain core, with nail and care gaining traction at Sally and innovation driving BSG. Promotions at Sally were more frequent but shorter.
  • Olivia Tong Cheang, Raymond James: Sought details on category growth assumptions and Q1 headwinds. Paulonis cited expected pressure on lower-income customers in Q1 from the government shutdown but confidence in the full-year outlook. She also discussed supporting stylists through improved app functionality for on-demand purchases.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on divisional performance drivers, store remodel success, and long-term growth levers, while expressing some caution regarding consumer pressure and promotional dynamics.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident and detailed, with Paulonis stating, "we are pleased to finish the year strong and look forward to making meaningful progress in fiscal 2026 towards the long-term financial targets." In Q&A, management remained constructive but acknowledged near-term headwinds and maintained prudence regarding guidance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone continued to be confident, reinforced by stronger operating results and positive momentum in strategic initiatives. Analyst sentiment remained focused on key performance risks but recognized the progress on innovation and digital strategy.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 guidance introduced a three-year financial target, offering more visibility than the prior quarter's focus on annual guidance. Management expanded on the Sally Ignited brand refresh, now covering 30 stores compared to 20 last quarter.
  • Growth in the color category accelerated to 7% in Q4 from 4% in Q3, with digital marketplace expansion also gaining momentum.
  • Management continued to credit the Fuel for Growth program with driving cost savings, now reporting $74 million in cumulative run rate benefits, up from $70 million last quarter.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from concerns over macro-driven softness in core categories to the impact of government shutdowns and the efficacy of new store formats.
  • Management's confidence was reinforced by improved operating performance, while analysts remained cautious about the consumer environment and the sustainability of growth drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited government shutdowns as a factor affecting lower-income consumer traffic in Q1 2026 and remains watchful of ongoing macroeconomic pressures.
  • Supply chain and cost of goods impacts from tariff increases were noted, with mitigation planned through vendor negotiations, sourcing optimization, and modest price increases.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of growth as Fuel for Growth savings begin to wind down and whether category and digital innovation can continue to drive traffic and sales.

Final Takeaway

Sally Beauty Holdings closed fiscal 2025 with solid sales growth, margin expansion, and strong cash flow, underpinned by strategic investments in store refreshes, digital innovation, and product assortment. The company enters 2026 with clear growth drivers—customer activation, digital value, and innovation—supported by the ongoing Fuel for Growth program targeting $120 million in cumulative savings. Management remains confident in its long-term growth trajectory, while acknowledging near-term risks from macroeconomic factors and a cautious consumer environment.
